title: To amend the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 to provide refunds with respect to certain dyed fuels that are exempt from tax and with respect to which tax was previously paid.
plain_summary: This bill lets people get their tax money back if they paid taxes on certain colored diesel fuel or kerosene when they should not have had to pay taxes. The fuel must be the special dyed kind that is normally tax-free. To get their money back, people need to show proof that they took this fuel from storage places and that it was supposed to be free from taxes. This helps fix situations where people accidentally paid taxes on fuel that should have been tax-free.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Ways and Means.

sections.title: Section 1  Refunds With Respect to Certain Dyed Fuels That Are Exempt From Tax and With Respect to Which Tax Was Previously Paid
sections.text: This section changes the tax code to give refunds to people who paid taxes on certain types of diesel fuel or kerosene that should not have been taxed. The new rule creates a special refund for people who can prove they took out eligible dyed diesel fuel or kerosene from a terminal (a fuel storage facility). These people can get back the money they paid in taxes on this fuel. Eligible dyed diesel fuel or kerosene means fuel that had taxes paid on it before under section four thousand eighty one of the tax code, and which is supposed to be tax free under section four thousand eighty two of the tax code. The refund payments will be treated the same way as other tax refund credits in federal law. The section also makes changes to other parts of the tax code to include references to this new refund rule. These changes will start applying to eligible dyed diesel fuel or kerosene that is removed from terminals on or after one hundred eighty days after this law is passed.
